text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist,  

Samuel Carter, DOB: 14-02-1991, Hospital No: KCH-20251834 NHS No. 9463827501  
12 Brookfield Mews, London, SE15 3DL

Thank you for your referral and accompanying notes. I reviewed this gentleman in our neurology clinic today with his partner present. He wears a smartwatch with fall-detection and automated alert logging; these data have been helpful in corroborating the timing of suspected events, particularly overnight or when unobserved. The most recent alerts correlate with episodes witnessed by family on 11/09, 18/09, 24/09, and 30/09 at home, and with a brief collapse at work on 16/09 without associated trauma. 

He has a history of generalised epilepsy, with onset in late adolescence, and previous EEGs demonstrating generalised spike–wave discharges. MRI brain (2021) was structurally unremarkable. Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per week, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. The smartwatch timeline is broadly consistent with their seizure diary entries, although two nocturnal alerts appear to have been false positives related to vigorous movement during sleep; these were not associated with postictal features the following morning.

He reports good adherence to treatment, facilitated by blister packs and a phone reminder. Current therapy is sodium valproate 1 g twice daily and levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. He has previously tried lamotrigine (rash) and topiramate (cognitive slowing). No recent medication side effects are reported beyond mild tremor on valproate, which he finds tolerable. There has been no significant weight change. Liver function tests and full blood count from August were within normal limits. 

Witness accounts describe his generalised tonic–clonic seizures as abrupt loss of awareness with bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic movements, tongue biting on two occasions this quarter, and postictal confusion lasting 20–40 minutes. The daily absences are brief lapses with staring and interruption of activity, usually less than 10 seconds, occurring more often under fatigue. The drop attacks have led to two minor knee grazes in the past month; he now uses soft knee supports when commuting. He does not drive. Occupational health have arranged temporary duty modification to reduce ladder use at work. 

The smartwatch fall-detection feature is configured to alert his partner if the event persists beyond 30 seconds; they have been advised to trigger emergency services only if the convulsion exceeds 5 minutes or clusters occur. He and his partner feel the device provides reassurance and a clearer chronology of events. I have suggested they export the raw timestamps monthly to accompany the seizure diary; our clinic can upload these to the record ahead of review. 

In summary, he has generalised epilepsy with persistent generalised tonic–clonic seizures, daily absences, intermittent focal non-motor episodes with disorientation, and occasional drop attacks. Although the short-term pattern appears stable, the overall burden remains clinically significant. We discussed non-pharmacological risk reduction strategies, including consistent sleep, alcohol avoidance, and shower rather than bath. They will continue existing medication for now. I have requested an updated EEG with hyperventilation and photic stimulation to assess current generalised epileptiform activity, and we will obtain trough levetiracetam and valproate levels before the next visit to confirm adherence and exposure. Should seizures remain at this level, we will consider adjunctive therapy such as clobazam or perampanel, and discuss vagus nerve stimulation at a later stage if appropriate. 

I have completed an Individual Risk Assessment and provided written guidance on seizure first aid. He remains under DVLA Group 1 restrictions and is aware of reporting responsibilities. We will review in three months, sooner if there is escalation, injuries, or prolonged events. 

Yours sincerely

Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
Identify explicit seizure frequencies mentioned:
- "he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per week" → 1 to 2 per week.
- "He has daily absences" → daily absences implies frequency of 1 or more per day; as a minimum explicit numeric interpretation is at least 1 per day.
- "focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks" → roughly 1 per 3 to 4 weeks, which is less frequent.
- "drop attacks" mentioned as occasional, no explicit numeric frequency beyond injuries; not higher than daily.
Rule: choose the highest frequency among multiple seizure types. Daily absences are the highest compared to weekly GTCs and less frequent focal events.
Seizure-free criteria not met; also letter says "Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported" but this is within a short timeframe and overall pattern unchanged; cannot infer 6 months seizure-free.
Thus final label: 1 per day (reflecting daily absences).
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per week, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
